<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun in Kōfu, Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} | name = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | native_name = 甲斐銚子塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kai Choshizuka Kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.592222|N|138.578333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|WD=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|WD=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=stone tools}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|WD=Q46492|lt=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|WD=Q6573293|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{template-inline|Kai Choshizuka Kofun}} *[https://www.city.kofu.yamanashi.jp/senior/bunkazai/024.html Kofu city home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/maizou-bnk/cyousiduka/cyousiduka-maruyamaduka-youkoso.html Yamanashi Prefectural home page] {{in lang|ja}}

The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|WD=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|WD=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River|1=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|WD=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|WD=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.

It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|WD=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | native_name = 甲斐銚子塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kai Choshizuka Kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.592222|N|138.578333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932|1=Kōfu}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|WD=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|WD=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River|1=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|WD=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kai Choshizuka Kofun]][[en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]][[ja:甲斐銚子塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun in Kōfu, Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | native_name = 甲斐銚子塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kai Choshizuka Kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.592222|N|138.578333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932|1=Kōfu}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|WD=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|WD=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River|1=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|WD=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|WD=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|WD=Q6573293|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)}}

The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|qid=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.

However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|qid=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|qid=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} | name = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | native_name = 甲斐銚子塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Kai Choshizuka Kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kai Chōshizuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.592222|N|138.578333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}} | region = {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kai Chōshizuka Kofun'''|甲斐銚子塚古墳|}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Shimosone neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Kōfu, Yamanashi|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|qid=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|qid=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River|1=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|qid=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|qid=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|qid=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|qid=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|qid=Q6573293|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Yamanashi)}}

Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

Together with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Maruyamazuka Kofun'''|丸山塚古墳| }} it was designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1930.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138487 |title= 銚子塚古墳附丸山塚古墳 |trans-title= |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> It is one of the largest keyhole-shaped tumuli in eastern Japan and has a total length of 169 meters. The tumuli are located a three minute walk from the "Kenritsukōkohakubutsukan" bus stop on the Yamanashi Kōtsu Kankō Bus from {{ill|Kōfu Station|en|Kōfu Station|qid=Q1066072|lt=Kōfu Station|1=Kōfu Station}}.
==Overview== "Chōshizuka" is a popular name for {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|qid=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumuli|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} ( {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=[[Zenpō-kōen-fun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}), which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shape of the tumulus from the side was thought to resemble that of a ''chōshi'', or long-handled ''sake'' bottle, and thus ''kofun'' named "Chōshizuka" are found in many locations. Thisi Chōshizuka Kofun is located in the southeastern edge of the Kofu Basin at an elevation of 260 meters, at the northern foot of the Sone Hills on the left bank of the {{ill|Fuefuki River|en|Fuefuki River|qid=Q11599751|lt=Fuefuki River|1=Fuefuki River}}. The area has a high concentration of ''kofun'', and this tumulus is part of the Higashiyama Kofun cluster. The tumulus has a three tier posterior circle, 92 meters in diameter and 15 meters in height, with a two tier anterior rectangular portion with a width of 69 meters and height of 8.5 meters. It was once covered in ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'', and was once surrounded by moat. It is orientated facing east. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in 1925, at which time a vertical-type stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was discovered in the center of the posterior circle. The chamber had an inner length of 6.5 meters, a width of 0.9 meters, and a height of 1.2 meters. The opening of this burial chamber in 1928 drew considerable attention due to the wealth of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} and clear connections to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of Japan. It had a thick [[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] coating on the walls and floor. Of especial interest were five {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, which had been made from the same mold as bronze mirrors recovered from the Bizen Kurumazuka Kofun ({{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}), the Sanbongi Kofun ({{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|qid=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}), and the Fujisaki Site ([[:en:Fukuoka (city)|Fukuoka]], leading to speculation that these were some of the 100 mirrors sent by Emperor {{ill|Cao Rui|en|Cao Rui|qid=Q378470|lt=Cao Rui|1=Cao Rui}} to the Kingdom of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}} in 238 AD per ancient Chinese records. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Other artifacts included a large quantity of iron swords, spearheads, and other weapons, {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|qid=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} and various items of jewelry, including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads. From these artifacts, the tumulus can be dated to the early Kofun period, around the late 4th century AD, and it can be inferred that the people who constructed it had strong ties to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. Many theories have been advanced as to the identity of the person buried in this tomb. One theory is that it is a person named "Mihitaki no Okina" based on accounts of the travels of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in this area during this time period as recounted in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Most of the artifacts recovered from this site are now stored at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
Further excavations were conducted in 1966 by {{ill|Meiji University|en|Meiji University|qid=Q46492|lt=Meiji University|1=Meiji University}} and in 1985 by the Yamanashi Prefectural Board of Education, and the surrounding area has been maintained as a public park. In excavations from 2001 to 2004, a protrusion was discovered on the west side of the posterior circle. This area was found to contain an unprecedented amount of wooden artifacts pertaining to rituals which were conducted at the site. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Maruyamazuka Kofun=== The Maruyamazuka Kofun is an {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun||}}) tumuli located about 80 meters east of the front part of the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. It has a diameter about 72 meters and height of 11 meters. The tumulus is a two-tiered structure with ''haniwa'' and a moat. It was excavated in 1904, earlier than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. At the top of the mound there was a 5.5 meter-long pit-type burial chamber which was found to contain a ''[[Shinju-kyo]]'' bronze mirror, an iron sword, an iron ax, a sickle, and other items. It is believed to date from slightly later than the Kai Chōshizuka Kofun. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
